Boretti, Alberto
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, we present a technology summary and update on the latest research advances in luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs). LSCs are optoelectronic devices based on a sun irradiation collector made of fluorophores that, after the solar radiation absorption, re-emit visible light propagating via a waveguide towards smaller area photovoltaic (PV) cells, which convert radiant energy into electricity. Here we review their basic principles, their performance analysis, and measurements, the various types of fluorophores so far employed, and the design and architectures proposed. We also include a brief review of the use of the energy transfer process and photonics to reduce their optical losses and improve their photons trapping efficiency. While their main application today is building integrated PV to meet future energy and emission targets, other envisaged alternative applications are also briefly summarised. The final performances in terms of electrical power conversion are linked to various fluorophores and device designs, while current limitations of scalability and stability are impeding their wider use.

head.ce:title: Luminescence solar concentrators: A technology update

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, we present a technology summary and update on the latest research advances in luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs). LSCs are optoelectronic devices based on a sun irradiation collector made of fluorophores that, after the solar radiation absorption, re-emit visible light propagating via a waveguide towards smaller area photovoltaic (PV) cells, which convert radiant energy into electricity. Here we review their basic principles, their performance analysis, and measurements, the various types of fluorophores so far employed, and the design and architectures proposed. We also include a brief review of the use of the energy transfer process and photonics to reduce their optical losses and improve their photons trapping efficiency. While their main application today is building integrated PV to meet future energy and emission targets, other envisaged alternative applications are also briefly summarised. The final performances in terms of electrical power conversion are linked to various fluorophores and device designs, while current limitations of scalability and stability are impeding their wider use.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: A Luminescence Solar Concentrators (LSC) [1,2] is a simple light energy absorber, converter, and concentrating device consisting of a thin slab of a transparent material of ideally high refractive index with embedded a low concentration of luminescent emitters (luminophores or fluorophores). LSCs’ emitters absorb a substantial portion of the sun radiation spectrum that is then re-emitted at longer wavelengths via the photoluminescence (PL) process. PL is a photochemical process that occurs in many optical and semiconductor organic and inorganic materials.

The system, in addition, to including fluorescent emitters in a host matrix, relies on the optical waveguiding principles and on additional perimeter photovoltaic (PV) cells to convert the fluorescent photons into electricity. The system is thus capable of transmitting the fluorescence photons from localized emitters by trapping them (approximately 75%) in the higher refractive index host matrix and guiding them along the plate by total internal reflections towards some of the perimeter edges, where the PV cells are placed. The emission not trapped is lost within the “escape cone” identified by the angle
θ
n
<
θ
c
=
1
Sin ( 1 / n )
,
with n the host matrix refractive index, generally ∼1.5. The photons are reaching the edge where the PV cells convert the collected light into electrical energy for its distribution to the utility loads (see the principle in Fig. 1(a)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Timeline of research on the topic
ce:section.ce:para: LSCs became scientifically significant from their inception in 1977 [1,2,7,8] till approximately 1988 [9], the field then paused due to the reduced price of oil and PV systems. It started again in 2000 [10] based on the advent of quantum dots (QDs) providing a red-shift emission and progressed very slowly till 2006; then from 2007 to 2008 till now new fluorescent materials have emerged (CdSe/CdS QDs and Carbon dots, C-dots) ( Fig. 2(b,c,d)), other PV cells were employed (CuInSe, GaAs), polymers were also studied and tested as host material [11], new architectures based on plasmonic and photonic structures [4,12] and alternative applications in buildings were identified [13]. A renaissance has been characterized by an old and simple low-cost technology with as yet limited or no technological maturity, nor significant commercial take-up [14].

The principle and concepts of the LSC are dated back to more than three decades ago [1] when the fluorescent material was based on dyes and laser materials, such as example rhodamine 6 G doped polymethylmethacrylate (PMMA) [2,8], followed by a series of experiments identifying the need of reduced optical losses in the host matrix including glass and emitters [15]. The motivation to study these devices was mainly the high cost of crystalline semiconductor PV cells based for example on Silicon, before the introduction of today’s low-cost thin film and third-generation PV materials [16]. As such the occurrence of the reduced cost of oil together with the technological advances in PV cells' efficiency and related cost [17,18] paused this area of research. However, later due to other emerging factors, such as the renewed relevance of renewable energy targets, the need of establishing new buildings with near-zero net energy [19,20], corresponding to annual energy consumption, which should be nearly entirely offset by the use of renewable energy, and the advent of novel luminescent nanomaterials [3,21,22], LSCs had witnessed a resurgence of research interest and growth of research again in recent years after 2007 with a significant increase in the number of publications [23].

LSCs can be directly connected with existing PVs, and do not require modifications of the PV electronics. The photoconversion in LSC allows tunability to better match the PV response and can offer new prospects for innovative product design ideas. Currently, research on alternative approaches to standard active PV collectors that resemble LCS is mostly based on the principle of spectral conversion due to the mismatch between the solar spectrum and high-efficiency PV cells with various bandgap materials [24] and transparent active PV materials [25,26]. In particular, the large range of colours, degree of transparency, and shapes of LSCs represent an attractive aesthetic benefit (Fig. 1e,f) for building integrated PV technology (BIPV) in the modern urban environment [6,13,27–29]. LSCs appear an attractive option specifically in cities with high population density and high-rise buildings concentration with limited land and limited rooftop space for sufficient PV installations to meet their electrical demands. LSCs are especially appropriate in cases where typical PV installation is not possibly identified for example in building with façades and windows that are north-facing, and other infrastructures as detailed in ref.s [13,30]. LSCs are considered a viable technology to convert finishing and windows in buildings' façades into electrical power generators as they can constitute also semi-transparent or transparent PV systems.

These possibilities have provoked additional development of the LSC over the past decade [21], witnessing an endeavour to move from small-scale prototypes [14] towards larger-scale demonstrations [5,31]. Start-up companies [32] for their commercialization have also emerged.

For these motivations, the concentration of sun light is not anymore the primary purpose of LSCs, and their research is now basically focused on the spectral conversion of sunlight to better match the needs of BIPV as shown in a recent comprehensive review [14].

This paper focuses mostly on the use of LSCs for solar to electricity conversion using PV. However, other applications have been recently envisaged [33,34] such as agricultural applications [35,36], medical diagnostic and telecommunication [37], optical communication [38], photochemical [39], and electrochemical reactors for water splitting [40], summarised in Fig. 2.

Examples of other emerging applications of LSCs as light resources for other photo-electro-chemical applications are solar‐driven electrochemical hydrogen evolution [41,42] and photochemical micro-reactors [43]. While the end use of concentrated solar energy may impact the specific design of the LSCs, the production of solar electricity using LSC with PV is by far the most widely considered use of LSCs.

Due to over 40 years of research on LSCs, several review papers and perspectives are available on this topic, where one of the foci is the choice of luminophores to enhance the LSC device efficiency and reduce optical losses. One earliest review of 2008, after the first resurge of the research in this field [15], summarises aspects related to the thermodynamic, ray-trace models, and experimental results based mostly on dyes luminophores and fluorescent nanocrystals, with a focus on issues related to LSCs stability. Later reviews in 2012 covered LSCs using II-VI QDs [22], Rare Earth Ions, and metallic nanoparticles [17], summarising about 30 years of research consisting of comprehensive LSCs efficiency tables for various materials, losses due to PV and waveguiding. More recently the focus has moved on the application in BIPV [13] and into additional inorganic nanomaterials promising better stability and tunability than dyes, such as Perovskites nanocrystals [44], and “green” quantum dots such as ZnS, CuInS2, CuInSe2, and their alloys (CuInSexS2−x), and indirect bandgap QDs such as Si QDs, C- dots [21]. The host matrix [45], polymers used in LSC [11], and the impact of various device configurations [23] have been also reviewed.

A recent critical review of the performance of LSCs points out some inconsistencies in the recent experimental demonstrations between light concentration and power conversion efficiency, evidencing the need to provide more details on the performance of the LSCs with similar approaches to be able to identify advances in the technology achieved with new materials and new designs or methods. Regardless of the many new materials tested, there is no clear power conversion efficiency increasing trend [14]. More recently the role of plasmonic in LSC has been reviewed [12] showing that there is no remarkable improvement in the device's power conversion efficiency. Due to a lack of a clear improvement owing to the materials employed as luminophores, recent research has focussed on the use of more environmentally friendly materials [46]. In the most recent commentaries and perspectives, the focus of this research field is to establish measurements and reporting protocols to benchmark the performance of LSCs due to the large variability of methods and some tests inconsistency in the literature [32,47]. There is also an attempt to reconcile various terminology of the literature regarding LSC external or optical power efficiency and the LSC electrical power efficiency conversion [48], as the optical and electrical efficiency of the LSC are often incorrectly reported or mixed. A recent perspective focuses on the most promising architectures of LSCs to overcome the low power conversion efficiency such as the tandem LSCs and fluorophores of recent generation, which could facilitate mass production at low cost and environmentally friendly large area LSCs [49].

We will focus here instead more on the recent LSC technological advances with the adoption of new designs and processes to determine which technology may be better placed to increase the impact of this technology in the future market.

An LCS is characterized by the dual purpose of photon spectral conversion and concentration: down-shifting of the sunlight into longer wavelength (lower energy) photons via the PL process, then guiding and concentrating the generated photons to a smaller area compared to the collecting area of the sunlight to PV cells.

Their conceptual operation also relies on some, in principle, other advantages compared to the direct conversion of photons to electricity from a PV cell such as the use of low reflection losses and focusing solar concentrator that can collect sunlight from a larger area and focus it onto a smaller area while circumventing the difficulties of as example the requirement to collect direct sunlight and its tracking typical of regular solar concentrators; the possibility to harvest the ultraviolet (UV) part of the solar spectrum and convert it to lower energy photons that can be better absorbed by as example lower cost silicon PV and a reduced thermal dissipation; the need of reduced area PV cells and better employability for applications in buildings. One of the primary advantages of LSCs is that they convert direct and diffuse illumination, while solar concentrators convert only direct solar irradiation requiring high-precision tracking.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modelling LSC optical efficiency, concentration, and power conversion efficiency
ce:section.ce:para:
Recently a more accurate model has been determined to find a physical limit to the actual LSC concentration factor C
[52] which takes into account the spectral shape of the absorption spectra of the fluorophores into the final optical power efficiency. The best performance for the LSC fluorophores can be achieved with an absorption coefficient of the collected radiation α1 = α(λ1) at the main excitation wavelength (λ1) much larger than the absorption coefficient α2 = α(λ2) of the emitted light at the central emission wavelength (λ2) [50].

Q =
α1/α2, defined as the ratio of the absorption coefficients at the wavelengths of the incident and emitted light, is a figure of merit or a quality factor of an LSC. Q can estimate the tendency to re-absorption. The quality factor has been directly linked to optical efficiency. A scattering correction β to α2 is introduced and this depends on other waveguide losses, and it is independently evaluated. For an LSC device architecture made of a planar fluorescent block with length L , width W , and height d , where the edges are made of two high reflectance mirrors and two PV cells ( Fig. 3(a)), an approximated analytical model for

In Fig. 3(b,c) some simulated values of the optical efficiency and concentration are provided for given parameters and LSC size, showing that they both degrade as soon as the length of the LSC is increased, depending on the quality factor Q . The model outlines the maximum concentration that can be achieved for a given fluorophore when the LSC becomes larger. As such high-quality factors are necessary for large-scale systems. This approximated analytical model has been compared with Ray Tracing Monte Carlo simulations showing an excellent agreement for LSC with L up to more than 1000 cm (corresponding to
G > 500
), PL QYs from 50% to 100%, and LSC Q s up to 1000 [50]. The analytical approach permits a quick evaluation of the potential performance of LSCs simply based on the optical spectra of the fluorophores and the geometrical dimensions of the LSC slab. In the case of the side walls not being perfect mirrors but rather waveguide/air interface an additional loss factor needs to be included 0.4 (≈1/2.53) [50]. This analytical model has been also compared to the experimental values of the optical efficiency as shown in Fig. 3(d,e) showing a very good agreement for various optical density LSC, where an optimal point is found. While the analytical model certainly provides a very good qualitative approach to determine the performance of an LSC, its full benefit could be found by experimentally testing it for different types of fluorophores and LSC optical density or optical absorbance [52]. For example, it has been found that the peak optical efficiency can be approximated by the product of

ce:section.ce:section-title: Key performance analysis and measurements standardization
ce:section.ce:para: In recent publications in the LSCs field, it has been concluded that there are several deficiencies in reporting their performance and inconsistent definition of measurement protocols. This makes it challenging to compare the performance of laboratory-scale LSC due to experiments performed or described in different ways, with variations in fluorophores, different light trapping materials, different panel sizes, surface or edge modifications, different type of PV cells, and their attachment to the guiding slab. In addition, two common approaches on how LSCs are considered either as a PV or a photonic device, inevitably lead to the use of different metrics for their characterization, for the first the power conversion efficiency and for the second the optical efficiency so only one value is reported.

It has been recently agreed by some groups that the complete LSC-PV system should be studied and reported as an integrated PV device with the same figures of merits for PV performance as such the power conversion efficiency (
η
LSC , PV
or PCE) should be measured based on Eq. (7), paying attention to the use of the LSC actual area [47].

The electrical LSC characterization should be performed under an AM 1.5 G solar light illumination of the LSC top surface by measuring the I–V curve of solar cells mounted at the edge whose spectral response is known in the same standard conditions.

The optical LSC characterizations should be performed by illuminating the LSC top surface with an intensity-calibrated light source and measuring the emitted light during exposure at the edge with an integrating sphere coupled to a detector [32]. The key parameter extracted is the external photon efficiency or optical efficiency

The above methods are used for lab-scale LSCs. To study the performance of large-area LSCs (>200 cm2), very limited experimental approaches are available, and they are typically tested in an outdoor environment [54,55]. Outdoor studies of large-area LSCs have demonstrated significant results that may impact their practical applications. However, these studies cannot provide detailed evidence on the mechanism of light propagation and losses as the common laboratory instruments are limited in characterizing large-area LSCs. Primarily to study large-area LSCs customized and high-cost large-area solar simulators and large-size integrating spheres are required and are not commonly available. Other approaches using common laboratory equipment and regional measurements have been developed [29] and compared to conventional methods for large-area LSC. As such for large area LSC the studies described above cannot be applied, a limitation to understanding their performances at scale and validating empirical models.

For large area LSC, to compare the performance in terms of electrical power of the LSC with a standard PV cell under direct illumination, the electric gain is determined as follows in terms of the

LSCs' performance under diffuse light is important to determine the potential benefits of LSCs compared to direct PV cells. For the use of LSCs as windows, it has emerged necessary to report the Commission Internationale de l’Eclairage (CIE) x and y colour coordinates of transmitted light using a solar simulator [56]. CIE x,y colour coordinates can be directly established from the transmission or reflectance spectra and obtained from spectrophotometers that are manufactured to include this option in their software. The inclusion of representative photos is also promoted to improve their aesthetic [6,57]. The photostability measurements are also key performance to consider for LSC and indoor and outdoor photostability of LSC with Lumogen R305 have been conducted [58], revealing the need for a better choice of incapsulating material more resistant to UV exposure which tends to degrade the intensity of the fluorescent dyes over time.
In Fig. 4 the measurement methods of the key performance parameters above described for LSC from small size to large sizes are shown.

Luminophores' high quality is a key element for producing LSC devices. They have several requirements such as: having a broad spectral absorption, a high absorption efficiency over the whole absorption spectrum, being stable at higher temperatures ∼50 ºC, having a high QY, and high Stokes shift with minimum re-adsorption, they must match the emitted photons to the spectral response of the PV-cell (≈1.14 eV for silicon), they must be soluble in the host matrix material, be non-toxic and cost-effective to make it commercially feasible and suitable for BIPV system.

A variety of luminophores have been used starting from organic dyes, group III-V QDs, colloidal QDs (engineered core-shell to reduce self-absorption), and group IV (indirect bandgap and/or wide bandgap) nanocrystals or QDs of Silicon (Si), Carbon (C) and silica, metal oxides nanoparticles (such as ZnO), semiconducting polymers and more recently perovskite nanocrystals and QDs. We will now summarise the pros and cons of each type of luminophore with the best performance achieved so far while referring to previous reviews detailing the number of publications using the specific luminophores for LSC.

Rhodamines, coumarins, and perylene(bisimides) derivatives are the most used synthetic organic dye types for LSCs. Rhodamines 6 G, and Coumarin 6 (also known as Coumarin 540), are the dyes used at the beginning of LSC studies, while the perylenes and perylenebisimides were first described for LSCs in the late eighties [14,17] and mostly used in the more recent LSCs publications. Organic dyes such as Rhodamines and Coumarins possess high QY ∼95% (from 78% to 98%) and good solubility in polymer matrices. However, they suffer from narrow absorption bands, small Stokes-shift causing excessive re-adsorption, high concentration, necessary for efficient light harvesting, causing severe emission quenching and most importantly they suffer from photo-degradation effect [58], and spectral mismatch with the PV cell attached to LSC. See as example Tables summarising the most used dyes QY and their absorption and luminescent emission peak wavelengths ranging from 400 to 620 nm depending on the Dye in ref [15]. In addition, recently they have been associated with a low sustainability index [32,46] due to their use in conjunction with non-environmentally friendly solvents (e.g. toluene) for their processing. One of the best results achieved using dyes and based on an uncontended measurement [48], was based on a tandem device with one layer containing Lumogen F Red-305 ( the most used synthetic dyes in LSC due to its QY∼ 98% [15]) and the other containing a coumarin dye Yellow-CRS040. The device is made of a diffuse backside reflector and four GaAs PV cells configuration. The power conversion efficiency (
η
LSC , PV
) of this device, was measured 7.1% with a
C =
2.5 at the European Solar Test Installation laboratories [59]. This is among the highest PCE so far obtained in LSC. The high PCE is here explained mainly by using tandem absorption covering a large area of the solar spectrum and most importantly the use of high-efficiency SCs. Using Lumogen F Red 305 dye and perylene perinone dye with silicon solar cells, the PCE was 4.2%, among the highest using Si [60]. Perylene dyes have shown higher Stokes shift compared to the other dyes [61]. Enhancement of up to 53% of the PCE in LSC was reported when organic dyes such as Coumarin dyes were coupled with metallic (silver and gold) nanoparticles and mixed in polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) nanocomposite films plasmonic LSC, however, no absolute value of PCE is provided as incorrectly reported in ref [23].
Natural dyes or proteins applied in LSC are Phycobilisomes, Chlorophyll, R-PE, mScarlet, and eGFP, they are generally much smaller devices (2 ×2 cm2) with PCE below 1% [46].

Thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) dyes have been used in LSC devices for efficient sunlight harvesting due to their charge transfer characteristics and twisted structures, showing reduced losses due to reabsorption obtaining a PCE of 2.2% with superior resistance to photodegradation [62].

Aggregation-induced emission (AIE) is a mechanism occurring in some luminophores that exhibit high QY even at very high concentrations. Tetraphenylethene is an example where AIE occurs maintaining a high QY even at very high concentrations. AIE has been used to develop multiple organic dyes (perylene diimides) transparent LSC that can harvest UV light with reduced reabsorption due to energy migration processes between luminophores [63]. TPA-BT (4,4′-(benzo[c] [1,2,5] thiadiazole-4,7-diyl)bis(N, N-diphenylaniline) AIE-emitters displayed a QY∼1 in the polymer matrix due to the large Stokes shift, showing in 100 cm2 LSC a PCE= 1.4% under standard sum illumination and using silicon PV cell [64] (see Fig. 5(a-d)).

QDs from various semiconductors have been introduced to compete with organic dyes due to their small sizes 10–100 nm and owing to a variety of advantages, compared to organic dyes, such as a tuneable absorption/emission with their size diameter, large Stokes shifts, higher photo-stability than organic-based dyes, even if surface oxidation in presence of oxygen and light has been observed and an emission dependent on the host matrix [65].

The main advantage compared to other QDs is that they exhibit a wide size/shape/composition-tunable absorption spectra from the ultraviolet to the near-infrared (400–800 nm) [65], better overlapping with the solar spectrum. Their emission has also narrow spectra with high absorption coefficients; they have high solution processability and photostability; their Stokes shifts can be engineered to provide a minimal overlap between absorption and emission spectra for example using core/shell colloidal QDs. For these reasons, these QDs are considered the most suitable for large areas LSCs.

However, they tend to have a lower QY compared to the dyes (0.1–0.5) [65], resulting in lower PCE and optical efficiency in devices compared to organic dyes. Cd and Pb chalcogenide colloidal QDs (CdSe, CdS, PbS) [66] have been utilized as they have a bright luminescence and a tuneable band in the whole UV–vis–NIR spectrum, showing in general a much lower PCE compared to organic dyes. For example, CdSe/CdS QDs combined with high-performing GaAs solar cells, the PCE was quoted at 4.5% in ref [17], however, no details are available on the geometric factors. By Stokes-shift engineering of CdSe/CdS QDs with huge shells, LSCs can be realized on a larger scale [67] removing reabsorption losses for device dimensions of 10 × 10 cm2 with PCE= 2.8% using glasses with sandwiched QDs and crystalline Si PV cells [68]. In regards to stability, it has been shown that CdSe/CdS optical properties (quantum yield and optical efficiency) stability with temperature is low as with increasing operating temperature they decrease significantly [69]. One of the major concerns of heavy metal QDs is their toxicity and their low sustainability index due to the use of toxic materials also for their synthesis [21,70]. This has paved the way to use green QDs such as Si or C dots as discussed below [71]. Additionally, the use of solvents such as toluene has been reported [65].

Perovskite nanocrystals are either fully inorganic or organic-inorganic metal halide structures. Due to their optoelectronic properties, they have been used as emitters in LSCs [44]. Zero-dimensional perovskite QDs have been put forward for application to LSC as they provide a small absorption/emission spectral overlap, higher PL QY, robust stability, and ease of synthesis, obtaining a PCE of 1.8% over 100 cm2 area [72]. They have a similar appeal to the chalcogenide colloidal QDs, with a similar spectral emission range from 400 to 750 nm, with more potential in terms of facile material engineering and outperforming them in terms of chromaticity, spectral tunability, and higher QY (60%) [73]. One of the primary advantages of perovskite nanocrystals and QDs is that high QY can be obtained conveniently through ligand modification or defect passivation without a core–shell structure. The perovskites nanomaterials dimensionality (0D, 1D, 2D) can also be used to control the QY and Stokes shift [44].

The first demonstration of LSC with CH3NH3PbBr3 perovskite in Polyvinyl Alcohol (PVA) under UV illumination yielded a PCE of 0.7% [74]. However, their absorption and emission peaks are very close limiting their performance in large-scale devices due to high re-adsorption [75]. In the wake to reduce re-adsorption a simple dip-coating method has been used to fabricate large-scale (100 cm2 with a G=50) LSCs with methylammonium lead bromide (CH3NH3PbBr3) perovskites QDs in combination with plasmonic interactions with gold nanoparticles, achieving a power conversion efficiency of 2.87% [73]. Another strategy to reduce re-absorption losses consists in using layered perovskite nanoplatelets with a tunable number of layers within each platelet. Very fast non-radiative exciton engineering in each nanoplatelet produces a large Stokes shift and a high QY yielding
η
opt
( λ ) =
26% and
C =
3.3 for LSCs with an area of 10 × 10 cm2 [76].

By doping the nanomaterial, the luminescence can be shifted due to forming radiative electronic transitions between intragap states of the dopant impurities; for example, manganese (Mn)-doped CsPbCl3 nanocrystals have shown low reabsorption in large-area LSCs [77]. Similarly, perovskite nanocrystals such as methylammonium lead trichloride (MAPbCl3) and Mn-doped MAPbCl3 (Mn:MAPbCl3) were applied to LSCs, where the Mn-doped nanocrystals due to energy transfer have a large Stoke shift from 400 to 600 nm, improving the LSC's optical efficiency with a silicon PV. Mn-doped perovskites have a broad absorption with high Stokes shift due to energy transfer between the exciton and the Mn dopants, retaining a high QY [77]. Br-based Mn-doped multiple quantum well, coated with Al2O3 to improve the stability, were used to fabricate large areas (40 cm × 40 cm × 0.5 cm) LSCs with a PCE= 2.42% [31]. Near-infrared emission in 700 nm has been obtained with improved stability of the LSC and QY as high as 99% for CsPbI3 with a triphenylphosphine post-treatment [78]. These appealing fluorescent emitters have also shown effects such as quantum-cutting fluorescence meaning an emission of two low-energy photons by absorbing one high-energy photon in Yb3+-doped CsPbCl [79].

As such the major advantage compared to chalcogenide colloidal QDs is the ease of doping and co-doping that can be used to improve the stability, increase the variable emission and absorption spectra, and increase the QY (>60%). In particular, the presence of quantum-cutting effects may open a new opportunity to advance the LSCs field with doubled QY and theoretically four times higher PCE than that of conventional perovskite nanocrystals.

Perovskite fluorophores however face poor long-term stability and photodegradation. Additionally, another key challenge is the need to replace lead-containing toxic perovskites in LSCs, using lead-free perovskites nanocrystals which could retain the high QY. Bi-doped Cs2Ag0.4Na0.6InCl6 double perovskites with an exciton emission and a QY= 40% were employed to make a low-reabsorption, lead-free perovskite 100 cm2 LSC, achieving an
η
opt
=
21.2% with a C = 2.7 [80].

QDs of ternary I–III–VI semiconductors such as CuInS2 (CIS), CuInSe2 (CISe) and their alloys (CuInSexS2−x or CISeS) [81] are another group of non-toxic materials with large Stokes shift, and do not need core-shell heterostructure. Their main attractive element is that they do not contain heavy metals and can be fabricated in large quantities via high-throughput, non-injection techniques using inexpensive precursors, hydrothermal synthesis, thermal decomposition, and solvothermal methods [82]. These QDs have a large absorption cross-section and are spectrally tunable, their absorption spectrum has almost a featureless profile that extends over the entire range of visible wavelengths with a weak shoulder at ∼640 nm (this can be tuned [83]), and as such is ideal for harvesting solar radiation). The emission spectrum closely matches the maximum absorption of the crystalline Si PV (around 960 nm) and they are colourless so well suited for semitransparent LSCs [81]. Due to their absorption/emission spectra, they are considered re-absorption-free fluorophores therefore they have the potential for large-area LSC high optical efficiency. Their PL QY can be above 90% [84]. One of the best performing LSC has been obtained from CuInS2/ZnS core/shell QDs which have a QY ∼81%, Stokes shift of more than 150 nm, and combined with a c-Si PV the PCE was determined to be 8.4% [85].

It is to be noted the PCE's highest performance strongly depends on the PV cell efficiency, therefore the
η
opt
is often quoted and some QDs demonstrations have shown high
η
opt
,
as reported in ref [48].

Other examples among the most performing QDs-based LSCs are a tandem architecture of CuInSe2 (bottom) and Mn-doped CdxZn1−xS (top) with a 3.1% PCE;[4] a multiple-interlayer CuInS2/ZnS QDs LSC device demonstrated PCE of 3.6% [57] using high-quality glass for 15.24 × 15.24 cm2, however, no concertation was reported. A list of the most recent LSCs based on QDs can also be found here [14], where the relevance of the geometric factor G and concentration factor C is also outlined as in some cases the concentration is below 1 in high PCE devices.

These results suggest that ternary eco-friendly I–III–VI semiconductors QDs show excellent optical properties including high QY and large Stokes shift, however, some main drawbacks are to be overcome such as a relatively poor photostability [86], presence of many defects that induce a variation of their optical band gap and broadening of the PL emission [87] and there is a need of an-depth understanding of their toxicity [82].

Another approach to engineering large Stokes shift is based on the use of indirect band gap semiconductors, such as Si, which has a low QY in bulk, while Si QDs show similar brightness to direct bandgap semiconductors.

Si QDs are estimated to have a long lifetime (hundreds of microseconds) and QY of 70% [88], and they emit in the near-infrared (830 nm). They are biocompatible, earth-abundant, and environmentally friendly. They have reported optical efficiency of 4% [71] and the PCE of 2.8% is at the level of other non-green colloidal QDs. One of the limitations is the surface chemistry's significant effect on the emission which can reduce the actual QY to less than 50%, the limited absorption due to the indirect bandgap, and scattering effects due to the high Si refractive index. Using Si QDs to form Poly(methyl methacrylate) nanocomposites, reduces non-radiative effects (with QY in the 55%) and scattering by limiting the QDs agglomeration in the active LSC film [89].

Other nontoxic and earth-abundant materials are C-dots, with a simple synthesis method based on a hydrothermal approach using low-cost precursors. C-dots are often listed as QDs, however, their properties are in between the ones of organic dyes and quantum dots, making them an appealing alternative and promising to overcome both main fluorophores issues such as toxicity and stability [90]. C-dots have also major differences from other QDs as they possess quite diverse physicochemical properties with abundant surface groups (such as amino, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups, etc.), which are relevant for their easier solubility with various non-toxic solvents including water. Similarly, to other QDs they also exhibit tunable optical properties and high QY. Doping C-dots has improved the QY [91].

Regardless of the lack of understanding of the origin of their photoluminescence properties, they have QY up to 94% [21]. They can emit in the blue-, green-, yellow, and red-region and are used in tandem LSCs [92] with controlled and eco-friendly synthesis [93]. See an example of LSC based on tandem C-dots and their absorption and emission spectra with QY achieving a final PCE of 2.3% in Fig. 5(e-i). C-dots have been found to enhance the photostability of tandem LSC devices based on CdSe/CdS QDs under exposure to ultraviolet illumination, prompting their use as an additional layer to enhance both the PCE and long-term photostability [4].

One of the drawbacks of C-dots is their relatively small Stokes shift, compared to other inorganic quantum dots, limiting the PCE to 2% for a large-area single-layer LSC (>100 cm2). Larger area (100–225 cm2) LSCs were fabricated using C-dots synthesized via a space-confined vacuum-heating approach using eco-friendly citric acid and urea as precursors. This synthesis method increases the Stokes shift permit to produce Gram-scale C-dots with QY ∼65% [94].

Yellow emission C-dots with a large Stokes shift of 193 nm, corresponding to nearly zero reabsorption, QY of up to 61% were synthesized and used in 10 × 10 cm2 LSC with PCE of 4.1%, among the highest obtained with C-dots or other QDs in single layer LSC [95].

Silicon-doped C-dots have shown a QY of over 90% synthesized by hydrothermal method achieving on LSC a PCE as high as 4.36% 2.5 times higher than undoped carbon dots [96]. In the large area (∼100 cm2) tandem LSC PCE up to 3.8% was determined [97]. Using in situ cross-linking organosilane-functionalized C-dots (Si-CNDs), high loading concentration above 1 wt% -generally used for other colloidal QDs to avoid concentration-induced PL quenching- and up to 25 wt% was to improve the solar energy harvesting in LSCs, however, re-absorptions is the main limitations for large areas LSCs, however, re-absorptions is the main limitations for large areas LSCs [98].

An example of Si QDs tandem LSC based on a Perovskite solar cell (PSC) is shown in Fig. 5(i-l). The Si QDs LSC consists of a front quartz slab, a PMMA ring, and a rear quartz slab. Si QDs suspension in organic solvent (ODE) is contained within the two quartz slabs and the PMMA ring, and the air side surface of the front quartz slab has a nanoporous PMMA anti-reflection coating (ARC) (Fig. 5(j)). The Si QDs have an emission peak wavelength at 690 nm and an absorption peak at 365 nm with QY ∼38.86% (Fig. 5(k)). After the Si QDs absorb UV light, the re-emitted photoluminescence propagates across the planar waveguiding structure to the edges and it is converted to electricity by a conventional Si PV cell. The remaining incident sunlight other than UV is absorbed by the back PSC. Design with and without air gap with an index matching oil instead between the Si QDs LSC and PSC are studied and compared to the bare PSC (Fig. 5(l)). It is shown that when the air gap is in place the PCE of the Si QDs LSC/PSC tandem solar cell is lower than the bare PSC for all Si QDs’ concentrations, while without an air gap, the Si QDs LSC/PSC PCE improves for low concentrations (below 1.08 mg mL–1). It has been concluded that the Si QDs LSC/PSC tandem solar cell without air gap, even if has an overall higher PCE, does not collect the fluorescence from the edge and not only collects the directly propagated not absorbed radiation but also collects additional red emission so the LSC works more as spectra converter than a concentrator. Conversely, the Si QDs LSC/PSC tandem solar cell with air gap shows an optical efficiency enhancement towards the Si edge but has an overall device lower PCE [99].

In recent years LSC emitters have been created with various new fluorescent materials such as perovskite, however, the focus is now to use more eco-friendly materials such as C-dots. ZnO QDs are considered eco-friendly and free of heavy metals, and Eu-doped ZnO, showing two photoluminescence peaks at 551 nm and 614 nm were employed in LSC showing a high optical efficiency due to their enhanced PL [100]. Copper iodide nanoparticles are considered eco-friendly, and have a large Stokes shift and ultra-high photoluminescence QY∼92.2%. When Cu4I6(pr-ted)2 nanoparticles were combined with C-dots in a tandem LSC the PCE could reach 6.3% [101]. Sustainable emitters can be found among fluorescent proteins, that are biologically produced, and are also edible luminescent biomaterials such as mScarlet fluorescent proteins extracted from Escherichia coli expression system, with a QY of 61% in a red spectral region. LSC based on mScarlet achieved a PCE of 2.58%, which is a state of the art of QDs-based LSC [102]. Nitrogen-doped graphene QDs are also eco-friendly fluorophores that could reach similar PCE of organic dyes [103].

While the emitters are one of the main focuses, attention has been given also to the choice and expansion of appropriate host matrix/waveguide materials, which need to have optical properties to avoid PL losses (optically transparent in the visible and near-infrared regions and with high refractive index n), chemical compatibility with the emitters so to disperse the luminescent species evenly, do not interfere with the QY or quench the emitters PL, easy processing for waveguide or thin-film fabrication, and stability in outdoor operation and high temperature.

A material matrix was initially glass due to excellent optical properties and stability, however, due to high-temperature processing, it is less favourable with many emitters. Usual material matrix shifted towards polymeric systems based on polyacrylates (PMMA), polysiloxanes (PMDS), or polycarbonates (BPA); less common are poly(vinyl alcohol)(PVA), Polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) [11]. Epoxy resins and polyvinyl-butyral (PVB) [104] have been described as host materials for LSCs for their high physical strength, photostability, and superior adhesion to a variety of substrates. PMMA is the most used host polymer due to its transparency to visible light, high refractive index (n ≈ 1.5) and it has high compatibility with a dye such as Lumogen F 305 Red providing the highest optical efficiency. PMMA's drawback is its poor solubility and limited photostability to high-energy UV. Poly(cyclohexyl methacrylate) (PCHMA) has been used as an alternative to PMMA due to its less polar nature improving the fluorophore Lumogen F Red 305 dispersibility in the matrix and LSC’s
η
opt
[105].

In addition to the polymeric host, hybrids of organic-inorganic polymers and multifunctional organic systems have been proposed [45]. Recent focus is also on more sustainable host materials such as naturally based polymers such as L-poly(lactic acid) (L-PLA), cellulose nanocrystal, and silk fibroin, characterized by similar refractive index, facile processing, and transparency of PMMA [46].

In addition to PL from various fluorophores, LSCs have been developed around other physical and chemical processes that can aid to reduce emission losses, enhancing solar energy harvesting, enhancing photon collection efficiency, or improving the spectral region of the photon to electron conversion. These processes are described in the following sections.

An effective way to reduce the re-absorption rate in LSCs is to decrease the spectral overlap between the absorption and emission spectra of the luminophores. However, this is relatively difficult to achieve as it requires synthesis engineering, and only a few of the fluorophores so far investigated are amenable to Stokes shift engineering and retain low toxicity, photo-stability, and high QY. To overcome this issue, FRET between multiple luminophores can be used and generally it is achieved with dyes but hybrid organic-inorganic approaches are emerging.

FRET is a non-radiative process of energy transfer from a donor to an emitter via dipole–dipole interactions occurring at distances of 1–10 nm. The use of the FRET system with LSC consists in having a donor luminophore absorbing the incident light and transferring the energy to a smaller quantity of acceptors luminophores used to broaden the absorption band of the LSC and to increase the Stokes shift (as shown in Fig. 6(a-c)). When the concentration of the donor is much higher than the acceptor emitter, the energy may be transferred through several donors before it reaches an emitter, known as the energy migration process [63] (Fig. 6(b)). The application of FRET to LSC is based on using pair of organic dyes as one of the key requirements to have energy transfer is the nanometric proximity of the molecules, therefore organic luminophores can be attached to the surface of inorganic luminophores (QDs) to form hybrid organic and inorganic FRET systems [106]. Among the best results on PCE using LSC and FRET are the following. A perylene diimide-based donor–emitter fluorophore pair was chosen to both improve the photoluminescent QY of the acceptor emitter LR305 and to reduce the luminescence reabsorption in PMMA (see Fig. 6(d-g)). The LSC was based on PSCs obtaining a power conversion efficiency of 2.6% [107]. By attaching Alexa Fluor 546 dye (acceptor) to the surface of the CdSe-ZnS core-shell QD donor (Fig. 6(h-i)) one LSC was constructed with a PCE= 2.87%, corresponding to a 28% higher PCE than the bare QDs LSC [108].

One of the main issue of conventional luminophores is their spectrally limited absorption which generally does not match or cover the entire sun spectrum resulting is poor light harvesting if compared to direct PV cells which have a spectrally broader solar spectrum absorption. The combination of conventional luminophores which are down-shifting the sun emission with up conversion mechanisms in the other emitters can result is larger spectral harvesting of the sun light. The effect of up-conversion permits to convert photons at lower energy (red) into emission at higher energy for example in the green, so it works in a complementary way compared to fluorophores that absorb photons at higher energy and re-emit them at lower energy (downshift). Up-conversion is an anti-Stokes type of emission, compared to Stokes emission of fluorescent materials. By combining an upconverter, absorbing red light and emitting in the green, and a down shifter, absorbing in the violet and emitting in the green, a dual-band LSC can be constructed. It results in being highly transparent and can better match the PV absorption of very efficient (17%) mixed halide PSC. The dual LSCs coupled with a PSC exhibit a high average transmittance of 82% in the visible and PCE values were up to 9.6% in 10 cm × 5.0 cm size [109]. Values of PCE of 8.77% were found in nitrogen-doped graphene QDs based on up-conversion photoluminescence and using a PSC [103].

Regardless of the many different materials adopted over the last 30 years in LSCs, however, the overall power conversion efficiency has not increased just based on using different fluorophores as their contribution can increase the QY and reduce re-adsorption losses via extended Stokes Shift. Rather future improvements may be more based on additional ways of improving sunlight harvesting and trapping (increasing the
η
trap
)
, reducing emission and optical waveguiding losses (thus overall increasing the
η
coll
) by using photonic nanostructures. The effect of photonics can also change the colour of the semi-transparent windows based on LSCs, a relevant decorative aspect in architectural designs.

To enhance the efficiency of LSC various photonic methods based on scattering and the addition of photonics structures have been shown to reduce the optical losses in LSC increasing light trapping and guiding.

By integrating scattering materials into LSC devices it is possible to increase sunlight harvesting by increasing the trapping of the sunlight within the LSC waveguide, reducing luminescent losses in the escape cone of the waveguide, and improving the directionality of the emitted photons from the fluorophores into the waveguide towards the edge PVs. The direction of incident sunlight will be changed when it irradiates the scattering particles increasing the interaction with the fluorophore and the probability of the light being absorbed by the fluorophores, while the longer wavelength emission will be better redirected by the scatterers towards the PV cells (illustrated in Fig. 7(a)). An increased PCE was observed initially using aggregated dye molecules achieving a PCE of 5.3% [110]. Silica (SiO2) microparticles were introduced together with CuInS2/ZnS QDs into LSC devices obtaining a PCE of 4.2% with 0.03% of 5 µm SiO2 particles corresponding to a 60% enhancement of the LSC with only the QDs [111]. Similarly using high-purity 4 nm TiO2 in addition to CsPbBr3 nanocrystals in PMMA, the PCE of TiO2-doped LSCs reached 2.62% [112]. However, the scattering effects can also induce additional losses if the concentration is not optimized as scattering losses from nanocrystal aggregation were found to become a limitation in larger devices [113]. Due to the limited use of scatterers, there is no sufficient understanding of the clear benefits of adding fluorescent guiding structures in the LSC, and modelling of these effects would be required.

Other photonic approaches consist of the integration of nanostructured photonic crystals with the purpose to enhance both the light trapping at shorter wavelengths and increasing the light waveguiding at longer wavelengths. Photonic crystals are wavelength-selective filters that can be applied to LSCs to reflect the luminescent radiation lost in the escape cone back into the light guide while better transmitting the incident sunlight.

Distributed Bragg Reflectors (DBR) are used to enhance the photon collection in LSC when used as a photonic band-stop filter on the top of an LSC. They were first applied using different dyes stack obtaining a 20% optical efficiency increase [114]. The luminophore is based on an organic dye (4-(dicyanomethylene)− 2-methyl-6-(p-dimethylaminostyryl)− 4 H-pyran, DCM). The DBR reflects the photons in the luminophore’s emission range that are not reflected by the guiding active layer, while it is transmitting photons in the luminophore absorption range, as shown in Fig. 7(c). DBR is fabricated by spin-coating onto a supportive glass substrate of alternating layers of SiO2 and SnO2 nanoparticle suspensions, the thickness and nanoparticles' refractive index contrast can tune the central wavelength for the mirror transmission and reflection, as shown from the mirror photo in Fig. 7(d) [115]. EQE of 23% was determined while the PCE was not quoted.

Another DBR based on an optimized one-dimensional photonic crystal combined with in-plane CuInGaSe2 (CIGS) solar cells is shown in the schematic of Fig. 7(e). The DBR is grown on the back glass substrate and is below the active fluorescent layer (Lumogen Red embedded in PMMA). The photonic bandgap reflectance is engineered to better match the Lumogen Red embedded in PMMA absorption or emission spectra. In this architecture light re-emitted by the dye within the escape cone directions or lost through the lateral edges can be harvested and guided to the PV cells. The DBR is a periodic structure with alternating refractive indexes of 1.95 and 1.37 of porous titania and silica nanoparticles layers, respectively. The integration of the DBR produces 28% higher light harvesting and guiding than without it [116]. The photonic crystal also changes the colour of the semi-transparent device as shown in the reflection and transmittance photos in Fig. 7(e).

The concept of photonic LSC (PLSC) has been proposed 10 years ago [117] via electromagnetic simulations. In PLSC the fluorophores are embedded in a photonic crystal to mitigate the escape cone losses and reabsorption, due to impeded emission inside the photonic band gap, thus the collection efficiency can reach 99.7%.

A PLSC based on CdSe/CdS QDs has been constructed, with the QDs sandwiched within variable refractive index layers of alternating Si3N4 and SiO2 [118] (Fig. 7(b)). The optical performance was enhanced by 40% due to the increase in the light guiding and a substantial decrease in the reabsorption losses when compared to traditional designs.

Another approach is to use photonic crystal slab waveguides with embedded QDs luminophores. The photonic waveguide absorbs light at photon energies corresponding to photonic crystal leaky modes, while the luminophores emitted light couples with the photonic crystal bound modes, a phenomenon which increases the light trapping. An example shown in ref [119] consists of hole-array and rod-array photonic crystals within hydrogenated amorphous silicon carbide (a-SiC:H) containing CdSe/CdS QDs (Fig. 7(f)). This structure can achieve
η
trap
higher than 92% resulting in a C at Eq. (2) close to 100. While light management improvement via photonics is promising and to a certain extent scalable, their effect in increasing the PCE has not yet been shown.

The large variety of materials and effects while they are not resolving yet the bottleneck of low PCE or concentration factor in a unique example, they however offer a lot of design freedom for their technological implementation for different end-users as shown in ref [120]. Different architectures of LSC are reviewed in ref [23]. and compared based on performances, however, the PCE is not consistently reported. We here remind of the most discussed designs trying to identify the benefits compared to the simplest most common design of Fig. 1(a).

The variety of designs used can, in some cases, improve large-size LSCs PCE, while mostly they provide better flexibility rather than better efficiency. An early design was based on cascaded LSCs, composed of a primary larger rectangular LSC based on organic dyes absorbing in the required sun spectral region, bordered by four smaller secondary LSCs with different dyes, having PV cells attached to their smallest facets. The secondary-LSCs contain dyes whose absorption matches the emission spectral range of the primary LSC. By the LSCs cascading the geometrical concentration of light is increased due to a lower area of PV cells [121], while the PCE is not improved.

In addition to a single junction design with solar PV cells attached to the edges, LSC devices with cells connected to the back of the LSC have also been explored. Bottom-mounted PV cells with edge mirrors were fabricated to enhance the PCE for LSC of increased size using dyes Lumogen Red 305 and Yellow 083 obtaining the highest PCE of 2.3% [122] ( Fig. 8(a)). Using Lumogen Red 305 and other colours LSC with PV cells attached to the back, referred to as Leaf Roof LSC, were prototyped [123]. Back-contacted solar cells show a better optical coupling at the interfaces between the waveguide and the cells, however, achieved a PCE of 5.8%, which is below the 7.1% [59] of edges PV cells and below the required threshold of 10%.

With the advent of micro-PV cells, they were sparsely distributed as arrays and embedded directly into the LSC with organic laser dyes or QDs; [124] this design allows the capture of light not only through the PV cells' top surfaces but also through their sidewalls and bottom surfaces for bifacial cells [125]. LSC systems were constructed with bifacial silicon and GaAs micro-cells embedded on the surface, so that they can generate power from the direct illumination and the wave-guided PL from the LSC [126] (Fig. 8(b)).

In addition to the simple single junction LSCs architectures to better collect the non-absorbed photons or photons lost in the escape cone, multi-junctions, and spectral filtering approaches are used. The same can be combined with PLSC as in ref [118]. A tandem InGaP/Si LSC with Si PV modules and top micro-cells has been constructed (Fig. 8(c)). The top is an InGaP III-V micro-cells array embedded into a poly(lauryl methacrylate) (PLMA) polymer waveguide with luminophores based on CdSe/CdS core-shell QDs. The bottom PV cell is a passivated contact Si cell. The LSC waveguide is encapsulated within trapping mirrors consisting of dielectric multilayer thin films to increase the light-trapping efficiency of the waveguide. The performance evaluation of the LSC/Si device was conducted at the National Renewable Energy Laboratory with outdoor tests under various irradiance conditions. This type of design is used to minimize the content of more expensive but high-efficiency III-V semiconductors PV cells while using the LSC ability to collect direct and diffuse light with no requirement for sun tracking [127].

Among the most performing in terms of PCE are the tandem LSCs as listed in the materials section, consisting of stacked multi-fluorophores layers LSCs, where enhanced performance is gained via the spectral splitting of incident sunlight employing fluorophores absorption tuneability. Namely, shorter wavelength absorbers/emitters are on top of longer wavelength absorbers/emitters. They have improved performance compared to single LSC for the larger area. They have been implemented since 2008 using organic dyes [129], and later using colloidal QDs(Mn2+-doped Cdx Zn1−x S/ZnS orange emitters on top of CuInSe2/ZnS QDs red emitters, PCE=3.1%, area>230 cm2) [130], C-dots and mixed-halide perovskite (PCE=3%, area 100 cm2) [128] (Fig. 8(d)), yellow and red emitting C-dots (PCE=3.8%, area 100 cm2) [97], green and red emitting C-dots (PCE=1.9%, area 100 cm2) [131], eco-friendly CuGaAlS/ZnS (CGAS/ZnS) core/shell (PCE=4.29%, area 100 cm2) [132].

After over 20 years of pause, the resurgence of LSCs has occurred due to different economic, environmental and scientific contexts. The advent of novel nanomaterials such as colloidal QDs, QDs of ternary semiconductors, perovskites nanocrystals, natural dyes, and green QDs (Si, C, ZnO, and silica) has contributed to a new hiatus for this research topic and their application in solar energy. These nanomaterials in some cases promise to be more photostable than organic dyes and present the option to engineer their absorption and emission spectra and as such the Stokes shift to reduce re-adsorption, using core/shell, other elements doping, and QDs size control. They however have some drawbacks such as increased losses due to scattering, not as high QY, and scalability due to additional optical losses. Some nanomaterials have also emerged with some low sustainability index issues if the use of heavy metals or toxic elements in their synthesis is present. As such so-called green QDs from abundant and nontoxic elements such as Si and C and natural dyes are preferred in the case if they can provide similar performances as the other QDs. More recently, new effects such as energy transfer and dyes aggregation are showing promise to increase the space of emission and absorptions spectral emission control, however, these effects have not yet been shown in the performance of actual LSCs devices in the current literature as generally, they are not very efficient.

It is expected that fluorescence energy transfer or up-conversion, currently studied for other applications, could be better used in a combination of new materials engineering in LSCs. The other strategy to enhance the optical power efficiency of LSCs is by improving the photons trapping in LSCs. Photonics approaches to increase spectral filtering and photons collection efficiency hold promises in improving the PCE and the concentration factors, however, no major improvement has been reported on devices' PCE.

The recent literature contains a substantial number of publications with sometimes unjustified claims of record performances, which have been also pointed out in recent reviews and perspectives. In addition, most of the demonstrations lack sufficient information on PCE, optical efficiency, concentration factor, and geometric factor, which are relevant to determine the advances.

To chase results based only on record PCE is not sufficient or it can even be misleading of the technology advances, as key device parameters such as the area and thickness of the LSC, the concentration factor, the geometric factor, and the device architecture (design and process) should also be quoted to determine if these advances are met.

Often, the concentration factor,
C ,
of some LSCs PCE reported values are neglected in many recent articles, whereas the most emphasis is placed on PCE. While high power conversion efficiency is certainly a priority, this objective should be pursued on devices of size and G values enough to ensure C values are at least equal to 1. However, because the concentration and efficiency reduce with the size of the LSC, the achievement record PCE is often accompanied by a considerably small (5 ×5) average size of the devices investigated (see Table 1). Therefore, in many “highly efficient” devices, the coupled PV cells produce less electrical energy with the LSC than without it. As such the focus of future strategies to improve the LSCs should not only be on the PCE but also on the size of the devices, mass fabrication of green fluorophores, and the measurement protocols used to determine the PCE [32].

The aimed application in BIPV, where the LSC is replacing the windows, requires a large G and large area. Additionally, for this specific application, transparency or chromaticity are important and this dictates the choice of fluorophores. It is difficult, given the lack of necessary information, to assess the technological advances that occurred for each type of fluorophore, and they appear to be scarce. The efficiency measurements reported in the literature have been recently questioned [47] due to a lack of consensus on measurement protocols and reporting standards which makes difficult the comparisons of various LCS designs, materials choices, and performances. In particular, direct comparison between lab-scale LSCs is not possible due to inconsistencies in the experimental measurements and reporting of the device and the necessity to focus on LCSs' optical efficiency has been pointed out to avoid reporting higher LSC device electrical efficiency by using high-efficiency PV cells [32].

In Table 1 we report a list of some of the best-performing LSCs demonstrated in terms of PCE compared to the area, G ,
C ,
and design to better identify specific fluorophore or design that has an overall better outlook. Other lists based on other criteria for comparison can be found in ref [23,44,84].

From the above conclusions and summary list, some specific strategies to improve the future fabrication of LSC can be provided. In general, the highest PCE (5–9%) can be found in small-size (5 ×5 cm2) LSCs where more than 2 fluorophores, higher PV PCE, and other effects such as aggregation and scattering or the LSC design may increase the final PCE.

For the single type of fluorophore, their synthesis methods using doped ecofriendly QDs, such as N-doped [91,94,133], Si-doped [96,134] C-dots and Eu3+-doped ZnO [100] have shown higher QY, larger Stokes-shifts, and higher photostability. Their synthesis methods are also critical to increase mass production [94]. Similarly other doping such as Mn2+-doped Cdx Zn1−x S/ZnS CuInSe2/ZnS [130]) or lead halide perovskite quantum dots such as Mn2+/Yb3+ co-doped CsPbCl3 with triple wavelength emission [135] can increase the overall brightness and QY with more than one PL peak, and due to efficient energy transfer processes, doping can reduce re-absorption, promising larger area applications. As such synthesis and doping methods of some of the fluorophores is still an important strategy to improve future LSCs. However considering the many efforts in this direction, these efforts should be combined with other strategies to achieve the industrialisation of LSCs. The combination of more than one type of emitters is also a good strategy in particular with other scatterers [111] or using donor-emitter fluorophores [107], even if this is mostly restricted to dyes. Physical effects such as up-conversion [109] could be combined with luminescence to increase spectral light harvesting and other fluorophores could provide up-conversion properties, however so far the performance metrics were overestimated due to improver characterisation protocols [136]. A less explored strategy is to improve the light trapping and simple, low cost and sustainable manufacturing of photonics filters or cavities could also apply more to this area of research if the benefit of high photons trapping is applied to LSC devices PCE.

For devices in the 10 × 10 cm2 and larger Tandem LSC, the PCE is generally well below 5%. For large-scale devices (currently >200 cm2), the lack of applicability of standard methods makes it difficult to study the photons' transport and loss limitations, however, the optical efficiency of LSC reduces dramatically with the size of the device, posing a serious limitation to the scalability which requires careful minimization of losses parameters for each design [137]. Generally, the tandem structures with hybrid fluorophores species or combined with up-conversion have a better outlook to improve the scalability with reasonable PCE. An element for optimization of the final PCE to be considered, which is often not discussed, is the index matching of the fluorescent waveguides with the PV cells and their attaching, which can constitute another relevant loss often not considered. Another element that is overlooked is the combination of fluorophores with waveguide materials and the final properties of the film as often only the fluorophores properties are considered in the modelling and not the combination of both in the absorption coefficient. Engineering higher refractive index LSCs is another strategy, for example using the concept of laminated LSCs based on a high density of fluorophores embedded in a thick layer and in between glass/polymers [84,138].

To improve the scalability a mass production of environmentally friendly bright fluorophores is necessary such as C-dots with various doping combined with improving their dispersion in the polymers [133]. Their surface functionalisation can also be used to improve their dispersion in the optical waveguide materials to accommodate higher concentrations without agglomerations and excessive scattering. In addition, methods to mass produce thin films of the fluorescent materials within the waveguide are necessary such as the use of the spray deposition method or inkjet printing method.

Another element that is often neglected is the stability in high temperatures and outdoor operation [58,69,127] as very few devices have been characterized in these conditions. A list of papers discussing stability under UV illumination can be found in ref [44]. Photostability under UV improved using encapsulation of the LR305-silica core-shell nanoparticles [139]. Encapsulating CdSe/Cd1−xZnxS in a Silica shell has permitted the demonstration of a 90 × 30 cm2 LSC area stability for 4-months photo-exposure tests and thermal stability tests at temperatures up to 200 ºC [140].

Given the limited advances in improving the PCE over large scales based on specific design and or fluorophores, the environmental impact and life cycle analysis of the LSC has become relevant to determine their applicability to BIPV. Ref [141] suggests that an LSC/Si tandem can have a significant environmental impact albeit lower than single junction technology at the end of its lifecycle. This is because the major factor contributing to the environmental impact is the Si PV which is energy more intensive and improving the optical efficiency and concentration of Tandem LSC/Si can lead to lower impact than using Si PV directly. However, the use of some Cd and Pb QDs can have high toxicity potential. While using C-dots [90,91,128] or graphene QDs [103,142] there is almost no toxicity or environmental pollution potential, using Cd or Pb-based quantum dots, the toxicity potential is high. It is envisaged that environmentally friendly QDs should be used in these applications for large-scale distribution if any industrial take-up could occur.

Finally, the most important strategy to advance the research and applications of LSCs towards their industrialisation, would require setting some measurements standard of their performance as it has been established for PV cells. Additionally, guidelines in the use of specific fluorophores in conjunction with a specific type of PV cells should be provided with the required level of transparency or chromaticity. Establishing road maps on what are the expected performances for various applications should facilitate their industrial uptake. Given areas, design, required transparency, and illumination conditions are to be set to be able to compare the performance of different fluorophores, as currently, the large variability of designs and measurement conditions makes it difficult to identify the best strategies to advance this area of research.

Stefania Castelletto: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Resources, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dr. Stefania Castelletto is a professor in Renewable Energy and deputy associate dean in mechanical engineering in the School of Engineering at the Royal Melbourne Institute of Technology University in Melbourne, Australia. She holds a Dottorato di Ricerca (Ph.D. equivalent) in Metrological Science and Technology from the School of Engineering at the Polytechnic of Turin (Italy). She has over 20 years of experience in research in optical engineering, metrology, quantum technology, and materials for energy conversion in both Academia in Australia and national research centers such as the National Institute of Technology and Standards (USA). Her current focus is on energy technologies.

Dr. Alberto Boretti is an Independent Scientist. He completed his Ph.D. in 1988. He has been a senior researcher and manager of applied research within the automotive industry for 18 years, and then for 15 years senior research fellow, associate professor, professor, deputy and acting head of department, and dean of research and graduate studies, within Universities in Australia, the United States, and The Cooperation Council for the Arab States of the Gulf countries, including The University of Melbourne, Missouri University of Science and Technology, West Virginia University and Prince Mohammad Bin Fahd University.
